description: A pairing session debugging a flaky test in a benchmark runner. No markers used.

chat.content: The reproducibility test is failing intermittently on CI but always passes locally. Same commit, same seed.
Same Python version on both? Different floating-point rounding can shift one bit.
Yes, 3.11 in both places. Locked.
Then the problem is probably non-determinism somewhere. dict iteration order? It shouldn't matter in 3.7+ but if you build a dict from a set anywhere…

Let me check. I grep for set( in the hashing path. Yeah, there's one — `set(brief['hard_constraints'])` then we hash the result. That's it. Sets have insertion-order-independent iteration but list(set(...)) varies across runs because of PYTHONHASHSEED.
That's the bug. CI doesn't fix PYTHONHASHSEED; you do locally.
Confirmed — local exports PYTHONHASHSEED=0 in zshrc, CI doesn't. Fix is to either pin PYTHONHASHSEED in CI or sort the list before hashing.
Sort it. Pinning hashseed leaks the workaround into every test.
Agreed, we'll sort. Let's not pin PYTHONHASHSEED globally — too easy to mask other latent non-determinism.
Want to add a regression check that no list-from-set passes through the hashing path again?
Yes, a grep-style test that fails if anyone reintroduces it. Add it to the determinism scanner.
Wait — before we ship the sort fix, do all callers actually expect a sorted list? If someone downstream depends on original order, sorting silently breaks them.
Hmm. I don't think anyone does, but I haven't actually checked. Let's flag that as a thing to verify, not block the fix.
Open question then: are there any downstream callers depending on original list order from that hashing function? Treat as unverified until we check.
While we're here — the auditor benchmark is slow on CI, about 12 minutes. Local it runs in 3. We never profiled it. Worth doing?
I suspect it's IO on CI rather than CPU but I don't have evidence.
Don't fix that now. Just note that the 12-vs-3-minute discrepancy is unexplained — we don't know why CI is slower.
Recorded. Anything else?

One more thing — we have an unresolved tension. CI wants fully deterministic, side-effect-free tests. Some integration tests intentionally hit the local filesystem cache. Right now we mark those flaky-tolerant. That's a smell.
Agreed it's a smell. We don't have a real answer yet.
